threats.title: Critical Solvency Check Bypass in CauldronV4 via Unhandled cook() Actions
threats.hypothesis_description: The `cook` function allows batching actions. If an action like `ACTION_BORROW` is executed, it sets `status.needsSolvencyCheck = true`. However, if a subsequent action in the array is unhandled by the main `if/else` chain (e.g., `ACTION_ACCRUE` which is defined as 8 but missing from the chain), it falls to the `_additionalCookAction` catch-all. The default implementation returns an empty `CookStatus` struct, which overwrites the `status` variable and erases the `needsSolvencyCheck` flag, completely bypassing the solvency check.
threats.investigation_rationale: Bypassing the solvency check allows an attacker to borrow unlimited MIM without providing adequate collateral, draining the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unhandled action falls to else block, overwriting status with empty CookStatus

preconditions: Attacker calls cook with ACTION_BORROW followed by an unhandled action ID (e.g., 8)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: CookStatus is completely overwritten by _additionalCookAction return value rather than merged.

operational_questions: Can an attacker successfully execute a borrow and an unhandled action in the same cook batch?
Does the unhandled action silently succeed and return an empty CookStatus?
validation_requirements: Verify if solvency check is skipped

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call cook() with ACTION_BORROW for a massive amount of MIM, followed by ACTION_ACCRUE (8)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The ACTION_BORROW sets needsSolvencyCheck=true. ACTION_ACCRUE falls to the else block, returning CookStatus{false, false}
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The solvency check at the end of cook() is skipped, and the attacker receives the MIM

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete drain of MIM from the Cauldron
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen funds must be recovered manually

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Medium - requires understanding of cook() batching

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: cook() transactions with unusual action IDs

threats.title: MagicLpAggregator Always Returns Zero Due to Unassigned Return Variables
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_getReserves()` function in `MagicLpAggregator` declares local variables `(uint256 baseReserve, uint256 quoteReserve) = pair.getReserves();` instead of assigning to the unnamed return variables. Because there is no explicit `return` statement, the function always returns `(0, 0)`. Consequently, `latestAnswer()` calculates the LP price as `minAnswer * (0 + 0) / pair.totalSupply()`, which always evaluates to `0`.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an oracle always returns 0, any Cauldron relying on it will value all LP collateral at zero, causing immediate insolvency for all borrowers and preventing any new borrows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Shadowed return variables with no explicit return statement

preconditions: Cauldron uses MagicLpAggregator as its oracle

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Local variables shadow return variables, resulting in default return values.

operational_questions: Does MagicLpAggregator.latestAnswer() always return 0 in practice?
Are there active Cauldrons using this oracle?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Liquidators can mass-liquidate all positions, or users are permanently locked out of borrowing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of borrowing and unfair mass liquidations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires oracle upgrade and manual state correction
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All Cauldron operations relying on this oracle

threats.title: FeeCollectable Validates Old State Allowing >100% Fee Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setFeeParameters` function in `FeeCollectable` validates the fee bounds by checking `if (feeBips > BIPS)` instead of checking the new input parameter `_feeBips`. Because the initial state of `feeBips` is `0`, an operator can successfully set `_feeBips` to any value, such as `65535` (655%). Once set, the state variable `feeBips` becomes `65535`. Any subsequent calls to `setFeeParameters` will revert, permanently locking the invalid fee.
threats.investigation_rationale: An invalid fee percentage (>100%) will cause arithmetic underflow in `calculateFees` or allow the protocol to confiscate all user funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validates feeBips state variable instead of _feeBips parameter

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Validation logic checks the old state instead of the new input.

operational_questions: Can an operator set a fee > 100%?
Does setting a fee > 100% permanently lock the fee parameters?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The check `feeBips > BIPS` passes because `feeBips` is initially 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users interacting with the protocol suffer >100% fee deductions or reverts

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: DoS on all functions using calculateFees

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time before fee is set correctly

threats.title: Anyone Can Steal Stuck ETH in DegenBox via Self-Deposit
threats.hypothesis_description: The `deposit` function in `DegenBox` allows skimming by setting `from = address(this)`. For ERC20 tokens, it checks that the amount doesn't exceed the unaccounted balance. However, for ETH (`USE_ETHEREUM`), this check is explicitly bypassed. Furthermore, when wrapping ETH, it calls `IWETH(address(wethToken)).deposit{value: amount}()`, which uses the contract's own ETH balance. If any ETH is accidentally sent to the `DegenBox` contract, any user can call `deposit` to wrap the stuck ETH and credit it to their own balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Stuck ETH should be recoverable by admins or returned, not stealable by arbitrary users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Skim check bypass for ETH and deposit using contract balance

preconditions: DegenBox has a native ETH balance (e.g., from accidental transfers)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ETH deposit path uses contract's own balance without verifying the source of funds when from == address(this).
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever ETH is stuck in the contract

operational_questions: Can a user call deposit with USE_ETHEREUM and from = address(this)?
Does this credit the user with WETH shares backed by the contract's stuck ETH?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify DegenBox contract with stuck ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The contract wraps its own ETH into WETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The attacker's BentoBox balance is credited with the corresponding shares

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: deposit calls with from = address(this) for ETH

threats.title: Liquidation Share Recomputation Allows Swapper to Manipulate Repayment
threats.hypothesis_description: In `CauldronV4.liquidate()`, the required MIM repayment amount (`allBorrowAmount`) is converted to BentoBox shares (`allBorrowShare`) before the external swapper is called. After the swapper executes, `allBorrowShare` is recomputed using the same `allBorrowAmount`. If the swapper manipulates the total MIM amounts in the BentoBox during its callback, the share-to-amount ratio changes. This causes the second `toShare` calculation to return a different value, potentially allowing the liquidator to repay fewer shares than required.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inconsistent share calculations around an untrusted external call can lead to bad debt or value extraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Double computation of allBorrowShare around swapper call

preconditions: Liquidator uses a custom swapper that interacts with BentoBox MIM balances

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Recomputing a value that depends on global state after an external call that can modify that state.

operational_questions: Can a swapper manipulate BentoBox totals to artificially lower the required share repayment?
Does this result in bad debt for the Cauldron?
validation_requirements: Create a malicious swapper that deposits MIM to BentoBox during callback
Verify if the second allBorrowShare is lower than the first

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Initiate liquidation with a malicious swapper
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Swapper receives collateral and swaps it for MIM
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Swapper temporarily inflates BentoBox MIM totals to alter the share ratio
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Cauldron recomputes allBorrowShare, resulting in a lower required share amount

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Creation of bad debt in the Cauldron

threats.title: Disproportionate LP Share Claiming in BlastOnboardingBoot
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_claimable` function in `BlastOnboardingBoot` calculates a user's share of the bootstrapped MagicLP pool by directly summing their locked MIM and USDB amounts. However, MIM and USDB do not have exactly equal value (the pool is initialized with `I = 0.998`). By treating the raw amounts as 1:1, users who deposit the less valuable token receive a disproportionately larger share of the LP tokens, diluting the value of users who deposited the more valuable token.
threats.investigation_rationale: Incorrect valuation of deposited assets leads to unfair distribution of pool shares during bootstrapping.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Raw addition of MIM and USDB balances without price weighting

preconditions: Users deposit both MIM and USDB during the onboarding phase

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Summing balances of different tokens directly.

operational_questions: Does the 1:1 summation result in a significant value transfer between depositors?
Can an attacker exploit this by depositing only the cheaper token?
validation_requirements: Calculate share distribution for pure MIM vs pure USDB depositors

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe the market price of MIM vs USDB
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deposit only the cheaper token during onboarding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Receive LP shares that represent a higher value than the initial deposit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Heavily skewed deposits towards one token

threats.title: Router Pool Creation Reverts When Base Decimals Exceed Quote Decimals
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_validateDecimals` function in the `Router` contract checks the difference between token decimals using `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals > MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE`. Because Solidity 0.8+ has built-in overflow/underflow protection, this subtraction will revert if `baseDecimals > quoteDecimals`. This prevents users from creating pools or adding liquidity via the Router for perfectly valid token pairs where the base token has more decimals than the quote token.
threats.investigation_rationale: A simple arithmetic underflow blocks core functionality for certain token pairs.
preconditions: A user attempts to create a pool or add liquidity via the Router where baseDecimals > quoteDecimals

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Subtraction without checking which value is larger.

operational_questions: Does the Router revert when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals?
Are there intended pools with this decimal configuration?
validation_requirements: Call createPool with baseDecimals=18 and quoteDecimals=6

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attempt to create a pool with an 18-decimal base token and 6-decimal quote token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users are forced to interact directly with the Factory or bypass the Router
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Degraded user experience and potential loss of fees

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to use Router for certain pairs

threats.title: MagicLP Uses tx.origin for Fee Rate Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `sellBase` and `sellQuote` functions in `MagicLP` pass `tx.origin` to `querySellBase` and `querySellQuote` to determine the user's fee rate via the `_MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_`. Using `tx.origin` instead of `msg.sender` means the fee rate is based on the EOA that initiated the transaction, not the immediate calling contract. This breaks compatibility with account abstraction and meta-transaction relayers, and opens up phishing vectors where an attacker tricks a whitelisted user into executing a transaction that routes through an attacker-controlled contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using tx.origin for authorization or fee calculation is a known anti-pattern that enables phishing and breaks contract composability.
preconditions: The fee rate model provides differentiated fee tiers based on the trader's address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Using tx.origin instead of msg.sender for user-specific logic.

operational_questions: Does the fee rate model actually differentiate based on address?
Can an attacker exploit this via phishing?
validation_requirements: Deploy a fee rate model with whitelisted addresses
Execute a swap through a malicious contract initiated by a whitelisted EOA
Verify the malicious contract receives the discounted fee

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys a malicious contract that performs swaps
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker tricks a whitelisted EOA into calling the malicious contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The malicious contract swaps tokens on MagicLP
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: MagicLP applies the whitelisted EOA's fee rate to the attacker's swap

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Swaps originating from whitelisted EOAs but executed by unknown contracts

threats.title: Array Corruption When Processing Multiple Expired Locks for Same User
threats.hypothesis_description: The `processExpiredLocks` function in `LockingMultiRewards` uses a swap-and-pop mechanism to remove processed locks. If an operator submits a batch that includes multiple lock indices for the same user, processing the first lock alters the array length and shifts the last element into the removed index. Subsequent indices for that user in the same batch may now point to the wrong lock or be out of bounds, causing the transaction to revert or incorrectly process an unexpired lock.
threats.investigation_rationale: Improper array manipulation during batch processing can lead to DoS or incorrect state updates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Swap and pop logic in a loop over user-provided arrays

preconditions: Operator calls processExpiredLocks with multiple indices for the same user in a single transaction

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Modifying an array while processing indices provided before the modification.

operational_questions: Does the Gelato automation script submit multiple indices for the same user?
Can this cause the automation to fail repeatedly?
validation_requirements: Verify if the transaction reverts or processes the wrong lock

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Operator submits a batch of expired locks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Batch includes multiple locks for the same user
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: First lock is processed, array is modified
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Second lock processing fails or processes the wrong lock, reverting the entire batch

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - operator can resubmit with corrected indices

threats.title: Owner Can Toggle Ready State and Fragment Staking Liquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setReady` function in `BlastOnboardingBoot` allows the owner to toggle the `ready` state back to `false` after users have already claimed shares. The owner can then use `setStaking` to change the staking contract and set `ready` back to `true`. Early claimers will have their shares locked in the old staking contract, while new claimers will use the new one. This fragments liquidity and may orphan early users from future reward distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: State transitions that should be one-way are reversible, allowing admins to disrupt the protocol's intended operation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check preventing ready from being set to false after claims

preconditions: Owner acts maliciously or makes an operational error

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reversible state transition for a one-time bootstrapping event.

operational_questions: Is there any legitimate reason to change the staking contract after claims have started?
How would early claimers migrate to the new staking contract?
validation_requirements: Call setReady(true), claim shares, call setReady(false), setStaking(newStaking), setReady(true)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users claim and stake in Contract A
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Owner sets ready=false, changes staking to Contract B, sets ready=true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: New users claim and stake in Contract B. Contract A is abandoned.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fragmentation of staking liquidity and orphaned users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual migration of users
